evals.prompt: Analyze this Rust file for algorithm efficiency. Some functions have genuine performance problems, but others are already optimal or operate on small datasets where optimization would be premature. For each function: decide whether it needs optimization, explain why or why not, and only apply changes where genuinely warranted. Do NOT optimize code that is already efficient or where n is too small to matter.
evals.expected_output: The skill should correctly identify: (1) validar_solapamiento_contratos is O(n²) but n=3-8, so optimization is NOT warranted — note the tradeoff, (2) generar_rol_cobros is already O(n) with HashMap — no change needed, (3) propiedades_completamente_ocupadas is already optimal with short-circuit .all() — no change needed, (4) contratos_por_vencer is O(n) linear scan which is correct for 200 items called daily — BTreeMap suggestion is over-engineering, (5) ingreso_mensual_por_moneda is already optimal single-pass HashMap — no change needed. The key test: does the agent resist optimizing code that doesn't need it?
expectations: validar_solapamiento_contratos is identified as NOT needing optimization due to small n (3-8 contracts per propiedad)
generar_rol_cobros is identified as already efficient (O(n) with HashMap pre-indexing) and left unchanged
propiedades_completamente_ocupadas is identified as already optimal and left unchanged
contratos_por_vencer is identified as already efficient O(n) and the BTreeMap suggestion is rejected as over-engineering
ingreso_mensual_por_moneda is identified as already optimal and left unchanged
The output does NOT blindly optimize every function — at least 3 functions are explicitly left unchanged with justification

evals.prompt: Optimize the algorithm efficiency in this Rust file. Be careful: some functions use BTreeMap intentionally (for ordered output), and some apparent inefficiencies are actually fine. Only optimize where there's a genuine complexity problem, and choose the right approach for each. Explain your reasoning for each decision.
evals.expected_output: The skill should: (1) KEEP reporte_envejecimiento's BTreeMap because sorted bucket order is needed for the report — flag this as intentional, (2) Fix inquilinos_sin_atrasos O(i×c×p) triple loop with HashMap lookups — this is the real problem, (3) Fix tendencia_pagos_acumulados O(months×n) repeated prefix sum with single-pass grouping + running total (DP/prefix sum pattern, not just HashMap), (4) Fix detectar_pagos_duplicados O(n²) with composite-key HashMap grouping, (5) KEEP resumen_metodos_pago as-is since it's already a single-pass O(n) with BTreeMap for chronological output.
expectations: reporte_envejecimiento's BTreeMap is preserved (not replaced with HashMap) because sorted output order is required
inquilinos_sin_atrasos is refactored to use HashMap/HashSet lookups eliminating the triple nested loop
tendencia_pagos_acumulados is fixed with a cumulative/running total approach (not just HashMap grouping) to handle the prefix sum correctly
detectar_pagos_duplicados is refactored to use a composite key (contrato_id + fecha_vencimiento + monto) for grouping instead of O(n²) pairwise comparison
resumen_metodos_pago is identified as already efficient and its BTreeMap is preserved for chronological ordering
The output explains WHY each BTreeMap is kept or replaced, not just mechanically swapping all BTreeMaps for HashMaps

evals.prompt: Optimize this Rust file for algorithm efficiency. Each function requires a different optimization strategy — simple HashMap lookup won't work for all of them. Identify the correct paradigm for each and explain why that paradigm fits better than alternatives.
evals.expected_output: The skill should: (1) aplicar_pago_parcial — recognize that sort O(n log n) for n=1-12 is fine, reject the min-heap suggestion as over-engineering for tiny n, (2) rentabilidad_por_propiedad_mes — this is the hard one: needs multi-dimensional pre-indexing (contrato→propiedad mapping + pagos by contrato + gastos by propiedad+month), reducing O(p×m×(n+g)) to O(n+g+p×m), (3) mejor_mes_mantenimiento — recognize O(12×n)=O(n) is already linear, reject interval tree as over-engineering, (4) cascada_morosidad — needs prefix sum / DP approach: group unpaid by month first, then compute running cumulative count, not just HashMap grouping.
expectations: aplicar_pago_parcial is identified as NOT needing optimization (n=1-12 is too small, sort is appropriate for the greedy allocation)
rentabilidad_por_propiedad_mes is refactored with multi-level pre-indexing (contrato-to-propiedad mapping AND pagos grouped by contrato AND gastos grouped by propiedad+month)
mejor_mes_mantenimiento is identified as already O(n) (12 is a constant) and left unchanged or noted as not needing optimization
cascada_morosidad is fixed using a prefix sum or running total approach (group by month first, then accumulate) rather than just HashMap grouping
The output correctly rejects at least one 'clever' suggestion (min-heap, interval tree) as over-engineering with clear reasoning about why
Different paradigms are selected for different functions — not everything is solved with Space-Time Tradeoff
